text.title: embedding-applications Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for embedding-applications. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: MTEB scores MUST be used as a starting point for model selection, not the final decision. Custom benchmarking on domain-specific data (legal, biomedical, code, specialized terminology) MUST be run before production deployment. Retrieval rank on MTEB can differ significantly from overall rank; agents MUST check the retrieval task score specifically, not the aggregate leaderboard position.
rule.rationale: MTEB aggregates 56 diverse tasks; a model that ranks #1 overall might rank #20 on retrieval if it excels at classification or clustering instead. Domain vocabulary (legal jargon, biomedical terms, code keywords) is often undertrained in general-purpose embeddings. Custom benchmarking with 50-100 labeled domain pairs prevents the costly mistake of optimizing for the wrong metric.

rule.statement: Custom benchmarking MUST be run on 50-200 labeled query/document pairs from your domain (not synthetic, not LLM-generated without validation). Recall@10 MUST be the primary metric; MRR and Precision@K are secondary. Speed benchmarking MUST use realistic batch sizes (32-128 texts), not single texts, because per-text latency drops 5-20x with batching. Benchmark results MUST be stored with model name, version, and timestamp so re-indexing is triggered automatically on model updates.
rule.rationale: Synthetic relevance labels are unreliable; human-labeled data (even 50 examples) is vastly more predictive than algorithm-generated labels. Recall@10 is the metric that matters for RAG (did the agent find the right source?). Single-text latency measurements are misleading; batching is always used in production and changes latency by an order of magnitude. Version tracking prevents silent degradation when embedding models change.

rule.statement: Vector insertion MUST use batch APIs with realistic batch sizes (100-500 documents per batch). Vectors MUST be normalized if using cosine distance (pgvector <=> operator, Qdrant cosine metric). The embedding model name and version MUST be stored in collection metadata; collection schema MUST NOT be changed after data ingestion (re-creation required). Distance metric and dimension size MUST be set at collection creation time and never changed without re-indexing.
rule.rationale: Batching amortizes network overhead and server initialization; single inserts are 10-50x slower. Cosine distance assumes normalized vectors; unnormalized vectors give wrong rankings. Model versioning is the only signal for triggering re-indexing when the embedding model changes. Schema changes (dimension, distance metric) after data ingestion produce silent correctness bugs — queries return stale results without error.

rule.statement: Agent orchestration MUST separate concerns into subagents: model selection via MTEB + domain benchmarking, embedding generation with batching and rate-limit backoff, vector database insertion with schema validation, and quality validation via benchmark_retrieval on held-out labels. A parent agent MUST gate promotion to production on Recall@10 > threshold. Subagents can parallelize benchmarking across candidate models; parent collects and compares results.
rule.rationale: Separation enables parallel work and clear success criteria. Model selection cannot be parallelized (sequential decision) but subsequent steps can. Quality gates prevent bad models from reaching production. Batching and backoff are table-stakes for handling rate limits and maintaining SLA uptime during bulk operations.

rule.statement: OpenAI embeddings MUST use batch_size <= 2048 (API limit) and implement exponential backoff on rate-limit errors (1M tokens/min on tier 1). Cohere embeddings MUST include input_type="search_document" or "search_query" parameter; omitting this degrades retrieval quality measurably. Voyage AI is preferred for code-heavy domains and multilingual queries. Open-source models (via sentence-transformers or HuggingFace Inference API) require GPU for reasonable latency; use only if cost savings outweigh latency budget.
rule.rationale: OpenAI rate limits are shared across the account; exponential backoff prevents account-wide throttling. Cohere's input_type parameter tunes the embedding for the retrieval stage; omitting it removes valuable signal. Voyage AI specifically trains on code and multilingual data. Open-source models have variable latency; GPU inference is required for sub-second latency at scale.

rule.statement: MTEB leaderboard aggregates 56 tasks; retrieval rank can differ significantly from overall rank — always check task-specific scores. Embedding API rate limits (OpenAI: 1M tokens/min on tier 1) will throttle bulk inserts; agents MUST implement exponential backoff or use Batch API. Vector DB schema changes require full collection recreation — agents MUST check collection config before upsert and fail loudly if mismatch. pgvector <=> (cosine) operator requires vector_cosine_ops index; wrong index type is a silent correctness bug. Embedding model updates (OpenAI silently versions models) may change stored vectors; agents MUST version collections and re-embed on deprecation.
rule.rationale: Silent correctness bugs (wrong index type, wrong input_type, stale vectors) are the biggest threat to production embedding systems. Rate limits, schema mismatches, and model versioning require explicit checks and fail-fast behavior.

rule.statement: Always normalize vectors before inserting into pgvector or other cosine-distance DBs using embedding / np.linalg.norm(embedding). Create 50-100 labeled query/doc pairs from real user queries (not synthetic, not LLM-generated) and benchmark against them before choosing a model. Use input_type="search_document" / "search_query" for Cohere and Voyage models; omitting this degrades retrieval quality. Store model name, version, and timestamp in collection metadata so re-indexing is triggered automatically on model changes. For OpenAI, prefer text-embedding-3-large with dimensions=256 when storage cost matters — Matryoshka embeddings retain most quality at 256 dims vs. 3072. Run benchmark_speed with realistic batch sizes (32-128 texts), not single texts; per-text latency drops 5-20x with batching. Set HNSW index parameters at collection creation time (m=16, ef_construct=200); re-indexing after data ingestion is expensive.
rule.rationale: Normalization ensures correctness with cosine distance. Domain-specific benchmarking prevents silent regressions. input_type tuning is a free 5-15% quality boost. Model versioning prevents data loss from model deprecations. Matryoshka embeddings (OpenAI's technique) compress high-dim embeddings to low-dim without accuracy loss. Batch size tuning is a 5-20x throughput boost. Index tuning at creation time prevents re-indexing costs.
